name: Manganese
name_origin: From the Latin 'magnes' meaning 'magnet', via the mineral magnesia nigra (pyrolusite, MnO2), which was confused historically with magnetite. The name shares roots with magnesium and magnetism.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Manganese is the 12th most abundant element in Earth's crust (~950 ppm) and the third most abundant transition metal after iron and titanium. It is found primarily as the minerals pyrolusite (MnO2) and rhodochrosite (MnCO3); vast deposits also occur as polymetallic nodules on the deep ocean floor.
states: essential trace element in living organisms

compounds.name: Manganese dioxide
compounds.summary: A black solid used as the cathode in alkaline and zinc-carbon batteries, in glassmaking to remove green tints, and as an oxidizing catalyst.

compounds.name: Potassium permanganate
compounds.summary: A vivid purple, strong oxidizing agent used in water treatment, disinfection, and organic chemistry.

compounds.name: Manganese(II) sulfate
compounds.summary: A pale-pink salt used as a dietary and agricultural micronutrient and in the production of dry-cell batteries.

compounds.name: Manganese(II,III) oxide (hausmannite)
compounds.summary: A mineral and a common product of high-temperature manganese oxidation; used in ferrites and as a coating for stainless steel.

compounds.name: Manganese(II) chloride
compounds.summary: A pale-pink salt used as a precursor to other Mn compounds and as a catalyst in some chlorination reactions.

compounds.name: MMT
compounds.summary: An organomanganese compound formerly used as an antiknock agent in gasoline.

uses: Steelmaking as a deoxidizer, desulfurizer, and alloying element (~90% of manganese use)
Cathode material (MnO2) in alkaline and zinc-carbon dry-cell batteries
Aluminum alloys (e.g., 3000-series) to improve corrosion resistance
Potassium permanganate in water treatment and disinfection
Pigments and colorants in glass, ceramics, and bricks
reactivity_summary: Manganese is a hard, brittle, silvery-gray metal that tarnishes slowly in air and rusts in moist air. It reacts readily with dilute non-oxidizing acids to release hydrogen and forms a wide variety of compounds spanning oxidation states from -3 to +7, with +2 being the most stable and +7 (as permanganate) the most strongly oxidizing. Powdered manganese burns in oxygen.
isotopes_summary: Manganese has only one stable isotope, 55Mn, making it monoisotopic. The radioisotope 53Mn (half-life ~3.7 million years) is used in cosmochronology and meteorite dating; 54Mn (half-life 312 days) finds use in radiotracer studies.

citations.source: CRC Handbook of Chemistry and Physics, 102nd ed.
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Manganese
